frontmatter.title: Stress and Strain: Elastic Properties of Earth Materials

children.value: Derive Lamé parameters from seismic velocities
Interpret strain measurements from geodetic observations
Use eigenvalue decomposition to find principal stresses
1. Introduction: Why Stress and Strain Matter in Seismology

children.label: 1. Introduction: Why Stress and Strain Matter in Seismology

children.value: Seismic waves propagate through Earth because rocks are
that deform under stress and recover their shape. Understanding the relationship between:
(force per unit area, Pa) — what causes deformation
...allows us to connect seismic wave velocities to material properties and interpret earthquake-related ground deformation.
2. Geometry of Forces in a Continuum: Traction and Stress

children.label: 2. Geometry of Forces in a Continuum: Traction and Stress

children.value: Before introducing elastic constants or constitutive laws, we must understand
how forces act inside a continuous medium
. Stress is not defined on volumes or points in isolation, but on
2.1 Traction: Force Acting on an Oriented Surface

children.label: 2.1 Traction: Force Acting on an Oriented Surface

children.value: Consider an infinitesimal surface element of area ΔA inside a solid. This surface is characterized by a

children.html: n^\hat{\mathbf{n}}n^

children.value: (\mathbf{t}(\hat{\mathbf{n}})) is defined as the force per unit area exerted across that surface:

children.html: t(n^)=lim⁡ΔA→0ΔFΔA\mathbf{t}(\hat{\mathbf{n}}) = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta \mathbf{F}}{\Delta A}t(n^)=ΔA→0lim​ΔAΔF​
t(−n^)=−t(n^)\mathbf{t}(-\hat{\mathbf{n}}) = -\mathbf{t}(\hat{\mathbf{n}})t(−n^)=−t(n^)

children.value: This orientation dependence is fundamental: the same material point experiences
different tractions on differently oriented planes
2.2 Decomposition of Traction: Normal and Shear Stress

children.label: 2.2 Decomposition of Traction: Normal and Shear Stress

children.value: The traction vector can be decomposed into:

children.html: tN=t⋅n^t_N = \mathbf{t} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}tN​=t⋅n^
tS=t−tNn^\mathbf{t}_S = \mathbf{t} - t_N \hat{\mathbf{n}}tS​=t−tN​n^

children.value: 2.3 Stress Tensor as a Linear Mapping

children.label: 2.3 Stress Tensor as a Linear Mapping

children.value: Experiments and force balance arguments (Cauchy’s theorem) show that the traction vector varies
with the surface normal. This motivates the definition of the

children.html: σ\boldsymbol{\sigma}σ
t(n^)=σ n^\boxed{ \mathbf{t}(\hat{\mathbf{n}}) = \boldsymbol{\sigma}\,\hat{\mathbf{n}} }t(n^)=σn^​

(σxxσxyσxzσyxσyyσyzσzxσzyσzz)\begin{pmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{pmatrix}⎝⎛​σxx​σyx​σzx​​σxy​σyy​σzy​​σxz​σyz​σzz​​⎠⎞​

children.label: 2.4 Worked Example: Stress Acting on an Inclined Plane (2-D)

children.html: (σxxσxyσxyσyy)\begin{pmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{yy} \end{pmatrix}(σxx​σxy​​σxy​σyy​​)

children.value: Let a fault plane be oriented such that its unit normal is:

children.html: n^=(cos⁡θ,sin⁡θ)\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)n^=(cosθ,sinθ)

children.value: The traction acting across the fault is:

children.html: t=σn^\mathbf{t} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}\hat{\mathbf{n}}t=σn^

children.value: The normal and shear stresses on the fault are obtained by projection:

children.html: tN=t⋅n^,tS=t⋅f^t_N = \mathbf{t} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}, \quad t_S = \mathbf{t} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{f}}tN​=t⋅n^,tS​=t⋅f^
f^\hat{\mathbf{f}}f^

children.value: is a unit vector parallel to the fault.
—is central to fault mechanics, Coulomb stress, and earthquake triggering.
3. Geometry of Deformation: Strain vs. Motion

children.label: 3. Geometry of Deformation: Strain vs. Motion

children.value: The two are related, but conceptually distinct.

children.html: u(x)\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})u(x)
u=(ux,uy,uz)\mathbf{u} = (u_x, u_y, u_z)u=(ux​,uy​,uz​)

children.value: , not absolute motion. It is defined from spatial gradients of displacement.
Example: A 100 m long bar stretched uniformly to 101 m

children.html: ϵ=ΔLL=0.01\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = 0.01ϵ=LΔL​=0.01

children.value: The displacement gradient tensor can be decomposed into:

children.html: ϵij=12(∂ui∂xj+∂uj∂xi)\boxed{ \epsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right) }ϵij​=21​(∂xj​∂ui​​+∂xi​∂uj​​)​

children.value: Only strain (not rigid rotation) produces stress

children.value: Even “pure extension” produces shear strain when viewed in a rotated coordinate system.
and eigenvectors play a central role in seismology.

children.value: The antisymmetric part of the displacement gradient corresponds to

children.label: The antisymmetric part of the displacement gradient corresponds to rigid-body rotation and does not contribute to stress.

children.value: Seismic waves, fault slip, and moment tensors all follow directly from these definitions

that link stress and strain in elastic materials.

children.value: Substituting into the P-wave equation and solving for λ:

children.html: λ=ρvp2−2μ=ρvp2−2ρvs2\lambda = \rho v_p^2 - 2\mu = \rho v_p^2 - 2\rho v_s^2λ=ρvp2​−2μ=ρvp2​−2ρvs2​

λ=2700×(60002−2×35002)=3.08×1010\lambda = 2700 \times (6000^2 - 2 \times 3500^2) = 3.08 \times 10^{10}λ=2700×(60002−2×35002)=3.08×1010

children.value: Typical crustal rocks have μ ≈ 30 GPa, comparable to structural steel!
For small deformations, stress and strain are linearly related:

δij\delta_{ij}δij​

children.value:
import numpy as np
from numpy import linalg as la

# Define 2D stress tensor
sigma = np.array([[s11, s12],
                  [s12, s22]])

# Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = la.eig(sigma)

# Convert eigenvector to azimuth (degrees clockwise from North)
azimuth = np.degrees(np.arctan2(eigenvectors[0, 0], eigenvectors[1, 0]))
When stress exceeds fault strength (~10-100 MPa), rupture occurs
Can trigger aftershocks where stress increased
